value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The 3-2-1 settled at $49.11/bbl, off $1.93 w/w. WTI itself fell hard — $76.54 front-month, down $6.81 on the week — but the crack still compressed because product settlements lagged the crude selloff, with gasoline leading the weakness even as the regime stays distillate-led. Yesterday's WPSR showed an 8.26 mb crude draw against a thin 0.91 mb gasoline draw and a 0.95 mb distillate build, a mix that did little to defend the gasoline leg. Watch: Whether refiners trim runs off 96.7% utilization if margins keep slipping into peak summer. Gasoline Crack: The RBOB crack settled at $45.77/bbl, down $3.42 w/w — the weakest leg of the barrel this week. With WTI down $6.81, gasoline values fell even faster, signaling the post-Memorial Day driving pull is underwhelming versus a 96.7% utilization wall of supply. The slim 0.91 mb gasoline draw confirms demand isn't tightening balances the way summer specs typically demand. Watch: Next week's implied gasoline demand print and whether builds resume against high run rates. Distillate Crack: The ULSD crack settled at $55.79/bbl, the firmest leg and the reason the regime reads distillate-led. The 0.95 mb distillate build is modest given 96.7% utilization, and with diesel exports running 1.51 mb/d, retained domestic length stays manageable — supporting the crack relative to gasoline. Freight and trucking demand continue to anchor the distillate bid even in the seasonal gasoline window. Watch: Distillate inventory trajectory as we move past summer toward early-fall restocking and any hurricane-season Gulf Coast risk. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $42.32/bbl, the lowest of the complex. Summer air-travel demand is providing a floor, but refiners holding strong distillate yields are capping jet's relative pull versus ULSD via the regrade. With the whole barrel repricing lower on the $6.81 WTI drop, jet tracked crude down without distinguishing itself. Watch: Regrade economics — if ULSD cracks stay rich, refiners will favor diesel yield over jet. Brent–WTI Spread: The spread settled at $4.05/bbl — narrow. Despite that, US crude exports printed 4.33 mb/d with products firm (gasoline 0.99, distillate 1.51 mb/d), so barrels are still moving — but a $4-handle spread signals the export arb is tightening at the margin, not blown wide open. With WTI down $6.81 this week, the spread already reflects relative WTI strength versus Brent; to re-widen and restore fuller export economics, WTI would need to fall further or Brent to rise. Watch: If the spread compresses below $4, watch for the 8.26 mb draw to reverse into builds as retained barrels back up domestically.
value.chart_321: At $49.11/bbl the 3-2-1 sits in the upper portion of its recent range — the trailing 20-week series ran from a 54.5 peak down to 43.5 before recovering toward 49–51, so this week's $1.93 pullback is a step down from the recent 51.04 high, not a breakdown. The crack remains comfortably above mid-spring lows, consistent with a healthy summer band, but the move is distillate-supported rather than gasoline-led — atypical for June. The driver this week was crude falling $6.81 with products not far behind, compressing the spread from the top.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack's $3.42 w/w drop to $45.77 is the standout weakness and undercuts the usual post-Memorial Day driving-season pull. A slim 0.91 mb gasoline draw against 96.7% utilization says supply is keeping pace with demand rather than balances tightening into peak summer. Summer-spec RBOB should command a premium here; the fact it's leading the barrel lower signals soft demand-side confirmation.
value.chart_dist: At $55.79/bbl the distillate crack is the firmest leg and defines this week's distillate-led regime — unusual seasonally, since June typically favors gasoline. The modest 0.95 mb build against 96.7% runs plus 1.51 mb/d of diesel exports keeps domestic length in check, supporting the crack. Underlying trucking and freight demand continues to anchor the ULSD bid even in the gasoline window.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack at $42.32/bbl is the weakest in the complex, tracking the broad $6.81 WTI selloff without distinguishing itself. Summer air-travel demand provides a floor, but with ULSD cracks rich, refiners are incentivized to maximize diesel yield over jet via the regrade, capping jet's relative upside. The spread to ULSD ($13+ in jet's disfavor) tells the yield-allocation story cleanly.
value.chart_bw: At $4.05/bbl the Brent–WTI spread is narrow, reflecting relative WTI firmness versus Brent rather than a wide-open export arb. Yet crude exports still printed 4.33 mb/d and product exports stayed firm, so flows haven't shut — the arb is tightening at the margin, not closed. With OPEC+ supply discipline and the 8.26 mb crude draw, a sub-$4 spread would pressure US barrels to back up domestically; re-widening the arb requires WTI to fall further or Brent to rise.
